speaker: interviewer
text: 1995, Take number three. If you could continue from the point of liberation where you went at that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Should I start talking?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: After three months-- that's 1942. That's approximately would be probably around May 1945-- May 1945. I was still in Miedzyrzecz. The war was still going on. I would say approximately-- I would say in Lviv. That's about 300 miles from [NON-ENGLISH] 345 miles from Miedzyrzecz. Was still the war going on. We could hear a lot of things. What happened there-- I was already fully in Miedzyrzecz. [INAUDIBLE] I took a house. I could take any Jewish house I wanted. The police made sure-- it was like a little government-- that I could take any house. But I wanted to stay near the police station. [? Munich ?] wasn't the police station. They used to come over to sleep over when it wasn't [INAUDIBLE]. What-- Then after that, I saw sooner or later, they don't want to have any witnesses. We were the only one, two Jews. They going to kill us, sooner or later. So I went away, and I spoke to the-- to the chief of the police. He made me up papers that I was born in Poland. I-- in [NON-ENGLISH]. It's not Poland. It's still Russia there because if you want to go to Poland, you've got to go up through close to Lviv. And then later on, maybe close to whatever you want to go. Lviv started already Poland. That's the [INAUDIBLE]. That's the [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] is [? Russia ?].

speaker: interviewee
text: He made me up papers that I could go away legally But he tried to talk me out of it. Why? There's a reason why. Not because-- it's a matter of fact I think one of his next-- the chief of police were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was talking to me Jewish. They needed more witnesses. They needed more [INAUDIBLE]. They wanted to have us there. I explained him. I said, I want to go quietly. Maybe in time, I'll go to Israel. I have relatives in Israel. Maybe I'll go to the states, or whatever. He says, fine. They give me some money and we went away legally. I came in with the train legally all the way Lodz, [NON-ENGLISH], by the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was still Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland. The war was still going on after. It was deep already. It was already in-- in close-- I would say very close to Berlin. It was-- they were fighting there like crazy. We saw the paper. It wasn't in much TVs, but we saw. So when I came in the first day, I went into the Polish government. It was already a Polish [INAUDIBLE]. They told us, go in that-- in that building. I went to that-- in that building. It was, I think-- I don't remember-- it was 16 or 17 Jewish people. And they give me and my friend-- no, my friend didn't go that time yet. My friend was still in Miedzyrzecz, by the way. He was still in the police. They would not let him go yet. I went away. They give me a room. I hear at nighttime, about 2:00 in that nighttime, screams. God, God, God, God, God, and screams. No shooting. I was lucky. I was in the second floor. I broke the window, and I hang down, and I jumped in the street-- people walking and everything. In the morning, I saw it in the paper. It was [INAUDIBLE]. That's the Polish gangsters, like the mafia. They didn't want to have any Jews. They didn't want any witnesses. They slaughter them with knives.

speaker: interviewee
text: Luckily, I think we were alive-- four people from these-- was it 16 or 17? I don't remember. I was one of the four people. So God was with me again. You know, I saw the death. If I wouldn't jump, they would probably break the door and go in and kill me. Then I saw it's bad. I don't want to go back to the building. Where will I go? Of course, when you talk Ukranian, it's very similar to Polish. I start asking, where do I go next? They said, the next station was Austria. You go to Austria. Of course, when you talk Jewish, you could talk German. I had a few dollars, already, you know, and rubles. You could use the rubles.

speaker: interviewee
text: I bought a ticket. I went towards the border. You know, they will not let you cross. I crossed by the stomach. Rolling there and there, I made the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in the border. I came to Austria. Where do I go? I can't go nothing there. I went away, and I slept in the stations, you know, in the train station. And I find out where to go to Yugoslavia. It helps the language. And what year was this or in what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1945. I went into Yugoslavia. Well, how did I go to Yugoslavia? I couldn't buy a ticket. I had no passport, no nothing. They wouldn't let us in. I swear, I went with cattle. It was a cattle train, and the cattles were making dairy, and I was squeezed right in between the cattle. I came all the way to Yugoslavia, Zagreb. I went in Zagreb. I was talking there to them, and think like that how to go to Italy. I don't know the language. Again, the same thing, too. I [INAUDIBLE] train, sneaked in. I went to Italy. I came into the border. They would not let you in. The same thing by stomach. At nighttime, to [? find ?] out, you crawl. In the morning, I came in the middle of the day time. It was four or five police catching me. The-- the Italian, wonderful people, they beat up the hell of me. They knocked me. I kept on saying, no, no, no, no. If I tell them I am Ukrainian, or Polish, or Jew, they will send me back to Ukrainia. I figured no way. I don't want to go there. Finally, they squeezed out to me that I could talk Yiddish, Jewish. They brought me a [NON-ENGLISH], and they told me the [NON-ENGLISH]. Even the [NON-ENGLISH] didn't tell them who I am. I said, I'm a lost person. I lost everybody in the war. And I start screaming and crying. They took me like a little coat-- it was nothing. And they decided they were going to put me in an international camp. The international camp was run by [SOB] the UNRRA. That's American-- just the United Nations. And there was all kind of nationalities. Of course, when they had me in the police, I was beat up like hell. You know, I was beat up, everything like that. In the international camp, it was a blessing. It was all kinds of people. I was-- that's calling Cremona. I was in Cremona almost the three months-- three or four months.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, they closed up the camp and they said, everybody could stay. I could become even a citizen. You could go any place you want to. How could you make a living? The international used to give us parcels, some of it. So I used to go. It was American soldiers. I used to pick up for them cigarettes, buy it, and go between the restaurants, and sell the package of cigarette that was unlegal. So I used to go from one city to the other one selling the cigarettes. After that, I used to rent a bike. I learned how to ride a bike, and I used to go out and sell clothing house to house.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, I saved a little bit money. I moved into an Italian home that they treated me, looked like a child. They had eight children. I couldn't talk Italian. That's the place I learned how to talk Italian. And that was a good life, you know, everything there. And then, incidentally, I went in. They made already a sanitoria for invalid people. I got a note of Jewish people. And it was run from the United Nations again. One man wrote a letter to Winnipeg. I never knew where Winnipeg is. I never knew what Canada is. I knew one thing-- that my parents are poor, and they're getting parcels each Yom Tov, Pesach, or Yom Kippur, and I knew this America. And I knew my mother used to say that she has three sisters in America. When they were-- when I said before they were orphans, the sisters went some to America and some, like I told before, the way my mother was a maid. And then I wrote down the name. But I didn't know the second name, but I knew my mother's maiden name was Zilberstein. Again, God with me, this auntie, what [INAUDIBLE], he knew-- she knew one of my mother's aunty, my mother's sisters, that her maid's name was Zilberstein. She wrote me the name. The people's name was-- my cousins were still there-- [INAUDIBLE] Sytno-- Jenny Sytno, my mother's sister. And the other one was Lina Sytno. And then in Boston was Golda, Golda Garner. And went away. And they wrote me a letter right away. And I should ask them-- I should write them back a letter in Jewish how I look, how my mother looked. Because a lot of people, they tried to get relatives, used to get money. As soon I wrote them a-- a sign, that my father was a crippled man, that my aunty-- wrote a few times my aunty-- they should leave the three children and come to America. My mother didn't want to leave him, my father, because they would not let in a crippled man to the America. My mother said she's in love with my father. She will not leave a crippled man. And that's why she was killed. Otherwise, probably she would be in Canada or Boston. Finally, I said it, and they believed me. I sent them pictures, and they sent me a little bit money. Was easier. And then they start making up papers. And that's the way I became to Winnipeg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you enter Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: April the 8th, 1948, I came to Winnipeg. Unfortunately, I had jaundice. I was sick [? in the shoulder. ?] They [INAUDIBLE]. I came to Winnipeg. I had $8 in my pocket. I-- my uncle offered me $10. I said no, I still have $8. If I have to spend the $8, I'll come. I worked. I went into a factory, and I worked as a foyer for eight years, [INAUDIBLE]. I start saving a little bit money. I bought a truck, and I went to peddle fruit. I used to peddle [INAUDIBLE] Italy, bring in batteries. I used to bring some scrap metal, sell it. After that, I was for a while going in. Bought a truck with another fellow buying cattle. After that, I saved a little bit money. I bought a grocery store. I sold the grocery store. I bought another little store. And then later on, in 1952, [SOBBING] I met my wife, Edith Lerner. She came from [INAUDIBLE]. I married her, a beautiful girl. Unfortunately, she didn't feel well. She had a lot of sugar, diabetes. She died in a heart attack. She give me three wonderful children. [SIGHS] I worked day and night, seven days a week, three jobs in the grocery stores, no help, no nothing. I made the best of it. Unfortunately, she got sick. And the doctors kept on telling me, because my middle son had a brain tumor-- he was born. He was-- he was 11 years old. He died. So I had at the time my wife in the hospital, my son in the hospital. And I had Two children-- Brenda and Archie Honigman. [SOBBING] And I used to diaper Brenda. She's right in here. The customers used to say like that, Sam, you make me the hamburger, I'll diaper Brenda. And up till today, there are some customers still remember me that way. After that, I was going on all the years like that. My wife died probably about six or seven-- about six years ago. She was very sick. And the only thing that I got out is two of the most wonderful children [SOBBING] that I ask in my life. They are with me all the way. They [? won. ?] [? Brendala ?] had to raise yourself alone, who helped me so much to raise up til today with your brother Archie. And up til today, he's your boss. They're doing very well. He's the one that assigns the checks. He pays your wages and even takes [INAUDIBLE], not the first time. He's very good to you, and God bless them. That's a blessing. So I would say I do have-- there must be a God. I'm not too fanatic with religion, but I do [INAUDIBLE]. It must be something, because I saw a lot of time and deaths in face, and so many times, I just get out of it. I don't know how, in what, but there's somebody's watching me. I don't know what does it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And right now, I have so much confidence in my two children that a lot of people say, why don't you get married again and again? I just can't disconnect me, my children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I tell Brenda-- she's listening probably-- [? Brendala ?], when you were born-- that's your birthday today. I love you when you were born. And Archie, the same, I love you. And I'll love you till I close my eyes, because they are with me all the way. [SOBBING] The best blessing what it could be, when they went with me to put in this headstone. If my son and my daughter would make me millions and millions and millions, wouldn't be such a good gift. [SOBBING] But they went with me hand by hand, step by step, to these steps with my parents. And I walked to that-- to that grave. Unforgettable. I'll never forget it till I die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain, if you could, what you did, and the headstone, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have an opinion like that. First of all, I think Brenda is a very good speaker. If you'll interview Brenda and Archie, they will tell you what a beautiful design. Unbelievable, that project, what they made. And it was done professional. And it's still there. It was done for-- not for my parents; for 3,000 Jews. When I walked around and I kissed the ground there, all around the grave-- Brenda probably remembers that. And I feel the thing is maybe it looked like that maybe I mentally, God forbid, I feel in my heart that I'm kissing my parents. [SOBBING] I need love. And I feel that maybe that's the spot where they were laying there. I took my handkerchief that I wiped my face. I can't help but to cry. I picked up some herb. And Brenda is listening. She says Dad, maybe somebody will question the border, maybe something [INAUDIBLE]. Number one, I said, I will fight with them. I will not give you that ground. I came home. I put it in a nice, beautiful, big container like for flowers. I put in a beautiful lightning around in my rec room. And each time, [SOB] the children know that if I want to have a good cry, I go down in that basement, and I look in that ground, and I put in that lights. And I remember all these things, that all the beautiful stones, and all the beautiful pictures all around there with Archie made it. Archie made a wonderful video with all this design. It looks very, very sad. But to me, it's a relief. I saw it. I can't get away with it. I never talked before so much to the children about it. I didn't want to hurt them. And I still don't want to hurt them. I'm keeping it to myself. But it's left a mark probably the rest of my life. They're saying you helped your child when I was 12 years old that left my mark that I can't help it not to cry, because I hear the voice, like I said, for this crying for these hours in that grave. And that ditch where the children listen to me, what I showed them that I laid in that little ditch. I would say, Archie knows better the measurement. It's probably no more than 20 feet from that grave, I was laying in that ditch. And my children will tell you again-- and it was coming out from Israel, 18 people. And we made all these headstones. We signed it, all our names in English, some of them in Hebrew. And we put in like a Torah right in there, like in the Jewish religion. And it's a big, beautiful headstone, and the fenced in with steel. You know, they could break it with a fence, with a gate, with all the beautiful flags, and all the beautiful flowers. And now I hear that the government is planting trees all around there. Before, they [INAUDIBLE] throwing garbage. And I feel that's the most wonderful thing. And I'm hoping in time that I'll go again saying the [INAUDIBLE]. And if the children will be in the mood to come, they will come. I don't know. Could be danger. But I'll take my risks talking about that thing. I feel my parents will pray to me. If they will think that I should get killed-- and I will tell you the truth. If it means to die, and if it means [INAUDIBLE], I don't know if somebody [INAUDIBLE]. I wouldn't mind if I get killed there. It doesn't bother me. It doesn't bother me. I have a nice memory, and it will be passed around through the generation. I got two wonderful children, you know. And that's life has got to go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it important for you to do this interview? What message did you want to get out to everyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message why I'm talking all this thing, because I feel like that if anyone-- just like you asked yourself-- do you want to be buried and forget about you, or you want to be memorized? So I feel because you're not living in-- in the states, that we are living here, that doesn't mean they don't have to be memorized. That's why I feel we should put in the names. Doesn't matter. Wherever it is. If it's in Miami. We have one in Winnipeg. We should put in to memorize. Like the rabbi said in Winnipeg to one of my cousins-- I asked him to come. He says, don't worry. He ignores it. He wouldn't go even probably to his own parents here to go to put in their headstone. You know, he put in the headstone, but he think that's all. I believe very highly, when I die, I would appreciate it very, very much if my children will come once in awhile as often as possible to have a look where I'm laying.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's respect for the dead people. The people don't-- can't talk. And why I feel that way too, because I'll give you a good example. My own family, my father's two brothers and one sister, all the family was nothing left to say put in a headstone over me. The 3,000 Jews, how many are left? They have nobody to talk to them. So I feel the same way, too. Some of the survivors, what they saying, it was 7,000 in Fontainebleau. They should go there, and put in headstones and see what they are there. And that's why they're using excuses. They said, it'll cost too much money. Why does a person works? Why do you have to do that? You know, you have to go ahead and honor that. They brought you in the world, and if you honor them, your children will honor you. So that's why I say the word that I appreciated what my children did for me. They went with me. They shared with me the sorrow. [SOB] They not just sharing with me the happiness. They're showing me the sorrow. I don't want to pass. I don't want to hurt my children. That's the last thing I want to do. But sooner or later, now I got older. I am 67. Sooner or later, I have to pass that on, like in another 25 years or 20 years-- how long does a person lives? Most of them, what I saw in Fontainebleau from the 7,000 are mostly in the 65 and 70. I talk to them there. That's why maybe you are here, because I said to them, wait a minute, bring over the young kids. Why do you-- why you scared to hurt them? Because sooner, in another 20 years, there will be nobody to talk to. You know, they wouldn't come around $7,000 young people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But if you pass it around, if you tell them-- you don't have to teach them. And some of them, they'll have a heart, just like you raise a child as a baby. You have to do that. You have to do that. And then they honor that. And I hope nothing-- we'll never see it again what happened. Enough what I went through. I don't want my children should go through that thing what I went through. That's-- you know, it's enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I feel very, very honored that somebody has to take care. I feel there's 3,000 people what we put on the headstone. It's like I said to you on the phone, it's my family. Because they have nobody to talk for them. Maybe there's a few of them left, but not too much. But even the few, I'm not saying that they-- they don't see eye to eye to do that. And I feel very strongly, just like anything else, it's very, very important to do that. And that why I don't need any honor. I don't need nothing. But it's very important that people should do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I spoke to the rabbi in the synagogue. And he said to me Sam, please, bring the video. I want to make a evening of it. That's the one right there, and we can do it there. And I probably sooner or later, I'm going to bring you the video, because there's a lot of Holocausters coming in that synagogue, and I'm trying to persuade him they should go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some of them say, wait a minute, just like they said in Winnipeg, is it safe? Are they going to kill? I feel it's safe. Even my children will say-- my daughter, Brenda, they're Canadian-- they will feel they didn't feel any added danger there. They were tourists.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Ukrainian people, they don't believe so much to break up stones. They will kill you, but they believe maybe they scared, forgot, or something, they will not break. Maybe they'll break up more on the States or in Canada than in Ukrainia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So far, it's already two years. We have guards, a contractor. They didn't touch. They only putting in flowers there. If I wouldn't do it, what would become there? What would my children say? [INAUDIBLE] we going to look at it? It will be growed up, covered with-- with grass, or herb, or garbage. It will be nothing to look like that. We have a memory. And that's the way I feel about it. OK. Is there anything else that you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say again, to me, what you did, Susan, and what's his name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Stephen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stephen, and Susan, I have no words for thank you that you picked me [SOB] in all the people. I don't know how many that you're interviewing me. And I offered you to pay for the video. You say no, you're going to get it free of charge. Like I went into Fontainebleau. I said it to you again and again, it was sold out, the tickets. Who did it to me, the-- the second time, the front, [? Esther. ?] She says, Sam, I didn't know what's going on in Fontainebleau. But they said it's $125 a ticket. I said to them-- to her, please, fund Fontainebleau. She said, you have no ticket. I said, I don't-- here's a good example, you have to have guts. I said, I'm not taking the word no. I got dressed up 1 o'clock. It's time 5:30, and I came out with a-- with a holler. And I came into that committee. And I said, here's the piece of paper. I want to join your people, to cry, the [INAUDIBLE], the 7,000. I'm one of them. I have to get in. I don't care the money. I went away, I put up a sign, wanted a ticket. They-- they went up immediately to the police. Was a lot of police. Was tight security. And they said, this is the man that we have to give him a chair. Doesn't matter what it is. And they're interviewing me. Maybe that's why you're here. You picked me, Susan, to come here. If I wouldn't do this kind of stuff, you wouldn't be here, and that's exactly what's is going on. You have to push yourself to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it's that drive that made you survive? Do you think it's because of that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not smart, but you have to have a drive. And I would say again, I am not a nice man, because if I would be a nice man, the way I walked with my crippled father [SOB] and my two beautiful children, I should have gone to the ground, to the grave. Why would I run? I had to have the heart, the guts, to do that. That's why I'm crying again and again. I had the guts, and I was lucky. I was lucky, and maybe somebody's watching me. Otherwise, how would I put in with my-- with the Israel people, and with my beautiful children, that beautiful memory would be nothing left. That's-- and I have to tell you, I'm thanking you very, very much. And I feel, and I wish you good health, you and your family, because you're doing a wonderful thing for the dead people, and for the live ones that like it says will never forget. And I believe it very, very much, we should never forget it

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hope you should give me your address and things. I want to still write you, and send you thank you, you and Stephen, very, very much what you did.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, thank you for doing this interview. [SOBBING]
